labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2B Negative feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2B Negative feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The prompt explicitly asks to distinguish negative feedback as a type of system feedback loop.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2C Positive feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2C Positive feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The prompt explicitly asks to distinguish positive feedback as a type of system feedback loop.

parts.content: Explain the concept of feedback in systems, distinguishing between positive and negative feedback.
parts.markscheme:
- Feedback is when the output of a system influences future inputs or processes within the system <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Positive feedback amplifies or enhances changes in a system <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Negative feedback counteracts or dampens changes in a system <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Positive feedback tends to move systems away from equilibrium, while negative feedback tends to maintain system stability or equilibrium <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks total</note>

questions.specification:
**Figure 1**: A system diagram of the global carbon cycle.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-ess-1350b917-f518-4054-b208-4ff8aef9c3f6-1771953681492/813334e1-c953-467d-a3ae-8209aeed4c1d.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.3 Biogeochemical cycles > 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is centered on a system diagram of the global carbon cycle, requiring identification of inorganic carbon storages and transformation flows, and impacts of warming on carbon storages.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) test systems concepts: steady-state equilibrium in the atmospheric carbon store and identifying the system type that exchanges energy but not matter (closed system).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.2 Climate change - causes and impact > 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) links increased global surface temperatures to changes in carbon storages (climate change impacts interacting with the carbon cycle).

parts.content: Identify **one** storage of inorganic carbon and **one** flow that represents a transformation in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
### Inorganic storage
<marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following:
- Atmospheric $$\text{CO}_{2}$$
- Ocean Surface (dissolved inorganic carbon)
- Fossil Fuel Reserves

### Transformation process
<marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following:
- Photosynthesis (conversion of inorganic $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ to organic biomass)
- Respiration (conversion of organic biomass to inorganic $$\text{CO}_{2}$$)
- Combustion (chemical change from fuel/biomass to $$\text{CO}_{2}$$)

<note>Do not credit Diffusion/Dissolution as this is a physical transfer (moving matter between phases/locations without chemical change).</note>

parts.content: Outline how a significant increase in global surface temperatures could impact **two** of the carbon storages shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant impact on a named storage, <marks>2 marks max</marks>:

- **Ocean Surface**: Reduction in carbon storage because warmer water holds less dissolved $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ (decreased solubility).
- **Terrestrial Vegetation**: Reduction in storage due to increased frequency/intensity of wildfires OR plant dieback from heat stress/drought.
- **Atmospheric $$\text{CO}_{2}$$**: Increase in storage as a result of reduced uptake by oceans/vegetation or increased release from permafrost/soils.
- **Fossil Fuel Reserves**: No direct impact from temperature, but may be reduced if warming leads to increased energy demand for cooling (indirect combustion).

<note>Credit can be given for any logically sound impact of temperature on a specific storage box in the diagram. Do not credit impacts on flows unless they are explicitly linked to a change in the storage level.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the atmospheric storage in **Figure 1** could be considered to be in a state of **steady-state equilibrium**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for a clear explanation:

- Steady-state equilibrium occurs when the total inputs to a storage are equal to the total outputs over a period of time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the context of the atmosphere, inputs include processes like respiration and combustion (which add carbon). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outputs include processes like photosynthesis and diffusion into the ocean (which remove carbon). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Despite constant flows of carbon into and out of the atmosphere, the overall concentration of $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ remains relatively constant/stable. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks max. The student must demonstrate the concept of balanced inputs/outputs maintaining a stable storage level.</note>

parts.content: State the name of the system type that exchanges energy but not matter with its surroundings.
parts.markscheme:
- Closed system <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept "isolated system" (no exchange of energy or matter) or "open system" (exchange of both).</note>

questions.specification:
The diagram below represents a sealed glass terrarium containing a plant and soil, which has been placed in a sunny location.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-1d0b11b5-0ecb-4994-bca9-a1bdafd15af7-1772167695732/86fe647d-2ffb-4132-94e4-8f2c895e37d1.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks to classify the type of system (open, closed, or isolated) and describe the exchange of matter and energy across its boundary, which are the core concepts taught in topic 1.2A Systems.

parts.content: Classify the type of system represented by this sealed terrarium.
parts.markscheme: Closed system <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Contrast the movement of matter and energy across the boundary of this system.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for the following:

- Energy: Energy (such as light or heat) can cross the boundary of the system/enters and leaves the jar. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Matter: Matter (such as water or oxygen) does not cross the boundary/is cycled within the jar and does not enter or leave. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not award marks if the student only mentions one of energy or matter.</note>

questions.specification:
Analyze the following graph showing a predator-prey population model:

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/383c9cc5-fa5d-4432-a428-af041b67ed0f)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.1 Individuals, populations, communities and ecosystems > 2.1C Ecological Niche and Population Interactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.1C Ecological Niche and Population Interactions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Predator–prey population model/graph is a classic example of population interactions (predation) and how each population affects the other over time.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for a limitation of the model in representing real-world interactions, directly targeting understanding of ecological models and their assumptions/constraints.

parts.content: Describe the relationship between predator and prey populations as shown in the graph.
parts.markscheme:
- Predator and prey populations oscillate over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prey population peaks are followed by predator population peaks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- There is a time lag between changes in prey and predator populations} 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

- The cycles continue indefinitely in the model <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>The total marks indicated in the markscheme is incorrect. There are 4 points each carrying 1 mark, hence the total should be 4, not 2.</note>

parts.content: Explain one limitation of this model in representing real-world predator-prey interactions.
parts.markscheme:
Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 2 marks:
- The model assumes a closed system with only one predator and one prey species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It doesn't account for external factors like disease, competition, or environmental changes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Real populations may not show such regular oscillations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The model doesn't consider the possibility of extinction or migration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It assumes constant reproductive rates and predation efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question directly addresses the concept of scale within environmental systems, which is a fundamental property of systems theory covered in this topic, including spatial and temporal boundaries.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly mentions 'models'. The importance of scale is a key consideration when constructing and interpreting environmental models to ensure they accurately represent the portion of reality they intend to simulate.

parts.content: Explain the importance of scale in environmental systems and models.
parts.markscheme:
- Scale refers to the spatial and temporal dimensions of a system or model <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Different processes may be important at different scales <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models must be appropriate to the scale of the system being studied <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Scaling up or down may introduce errors or omit important factors <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Understanding scale helps in applying model results to real-world situations <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 3 marks:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2F Resilience of a system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2F Resilience of a system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks about the concept of resilience as it applies to environmental systems, which directly matches the systems sub-topic on resilience.

parts.content: Describe how the concept of resilience applies to environmental systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Resilience is the ability of a system to absorb disturbances and maintain its basic structure and function <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resilient systems can return to equilibrium after perturbations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resilience involves feedback mechanisms and system adaptability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High biodiversity often contributes to ecosystem resilience <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Understanding resilience is important for managing and conserving ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 3 marks

questions.specification:
There are increasing concerns of carbon dioxide emissions and its relationship to climate change.

**Figure 1: CO2 emissions of select countries in 2007 and 2030 (Projected)**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/47aa86b5-8831-48f8-a160-711829ef964f)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.3 Climate change - mitigation and adaptation > 6.3A Mitigation and adaptation strategies
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.3A Mitigation and adaptation strategies
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b), (d), and (e) explicitly ask about mitigation vs adaptation and examples of strategies to reduce impacts and emissions.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.2 Climate change - causes and impact > 6.2A Climate and anthropogenic GHG emissions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.2A Climate and anthropogenic GHG emissions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (c) concern anthropogenic CO2 emissions trends by country and how CO2 (a GHG) drives climate change.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.1 Introduction to the atmosphere  > 6.1C Greenhouse effect
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.1C Greenhouse effect
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for the mechanism by which increased CO2 changes global climate, i.e., enhanced greenhouse effect.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.2 Climate change - causes and impact > 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Overall framing is climate change causes/impacts and emissions projections; connects CO2 emissions to global climate change broadly.

parts.content: Identify **two** possible reasons for China's projected change in CO2 emissions.
parts.markscheme:
- Growing number of fossil-fuelled vehicles/transport <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rapidly advancing economy/standard of living <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increase in fossil-fuelled power plants / increased industrialization <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increase in intensive/mechanized farming systems (in place of traditional) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Burning of forests to clear land for agriculture <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept any other reasonable suggestions. Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct reason identified, up to <marks>2 max</marks></note>

parts.content: Discuss how the ability to implement mitigation and adaptation strategies may vary from one country to another.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct explanation, up to <marks>4 max</marks>.

- Political will/pressure for change may differ due to some countries being more/less committed to their industrial lifestyles/economic growth / inhibited through political corruption
- Finance/economics may/may not allow some countries to fund new technologies/infrastructures
- Some countries may depend upon others for knowledge transfer/technological assistance to implement resolutions
- Religious/political/cultural norms/education in some countries may promote/limit their perception of environmental threats/approach to management
- Geographical location of some countries may place them at greater/more immediate risk from impacts of climate change (_e.g._ low-lying islands/tropical storm-prone nations) / or offer them greater opportunities for mitigation (_e.g._ available sources of alternative energy)
- Some countries may perceive greater immediate priorities _e.g._ war in Syria/poverty in Somalia


<note>Accept other valid explanations of equivalent validity.</note>
<note>Do not accept e.g. "Economics" alone without an explanatory note.</note>

parts.content: Outline how CO2 emissions may cause a change in the global climate.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following (<marks>2 marks max</marks>):

-  CO2 emissions (lead to higher concentration) of CO2 which is a greenhouse gas <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Causing greater absorption of infra-red/heat radiation and rise in global temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Leading to increased evaporation/changing winds/shifting patterns of precipitation/droughts/extreme weather events/storms/hurricanes/El Niño. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify **one** adaptation strategy that can be used to reduce the impacts of climate change.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for any of the following:

- Flood defences (_e.g._ levees/dikes) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Desalinization plants to replace freshwater losses <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Planting of crops in previously unsuitable areas <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water conservation (_e.g._ restrictions on use of irrigation/sprinklers) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Exploiting areas that have become more productive for crops through climate change <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Developing (_e.g._ drought-resistant) crops better adapted to areas impacted by climate change <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Green roof system that cools the building through evapotranspiration/reflection <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept any other reasonable suggestions.</note>  
<note>Some strategies can be acknowledged as both adaptive and mitigating e.g. a "green roof" both reduces impact of climate change by cooling the building (adaptation) and reduces cause of climate change by reducing C emission (mitigation). Such suggestions should not be credited unless the link to reducing impact is made clear.</note>

parts.content: Identify **one** reduction strategy the United States might use to achieve its projected change in CO2 emissions.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for any of the following:

- Implementing carbon pricing/taxes on fossil fuel emissions to incentivize a shift towards renewable energy sources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increasing investment and subsidies for renewable energy sources like solar, wind, and hydroelectric power <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- Promoting energy efficiency measures in industries and buildings through regulations and incentives <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transitioning towards electric vehicles and improving public transportation infrastructure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Implementing carbon capture and storage (CCS) technologies in power plants and industrial facilities <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encouraging reforestation and afforestation programs to increase carbon sinks <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept any other reasonable suggestions explicitly linked to reducing carbon emissions.</note>

questions.specification:
Analyze the following food web diagram:

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f4f33c7f-dd1d-4a67-891b-1e3eda7603b9)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.2 Energy and biomass in ecosystems > 2.2F Food Web and Biomass
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.2F Food Web and Biomass
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify a producer and consumer within a food web and explain how the diagram represents energy flow—core understanding of food webs and trophic relationships.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.2 Energy and biomass in ecosystems > 2.2D Food Chain and Energy Loss
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.2D Food Chain and Energy Loss
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Explaining how the diagram represents energy flow in an ecosystem involves direction of energy transfer between trophic levels and associated losses along food chains embedded in the web.

parts.content: Identify one producer and one consumer in this food web.
parts.markscheme: Award <marks>1 mark </marks> for correctly identifying a producer and <marks> 1 mark </marks> for correctly identifying a consumer based on the diagram provided. (Producer: phytoplankton) (consumer: Micronekton)

parts.content: Explain how this diagram represents energy flow in an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 2 marks:
- Arrows represent the direction of energy transfer
- Energy flows from producers to consumers
- Each trophic level represents a step in energy transfer
- Decomposers recycle energy and nutrients back into the system

questions.specification:
**Figure 1: A systems diagram of a pond ecosystem**

![Pond System Diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-62719842-4945-4c81-a72d-b06ff9b1ae20-1772167695732/8bc71f09-f098-4e9a-abf5-fad601e74945.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question directly assesses fundamental systems concepts including identifying storages of matter, distinguishing between transfer and transformation processes, and defining open systems based on a provided diagram.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) specifically asks for the advantages of using models to study environmental systems, which is the core focus of this sub-topic.

parts.content: Referring to Figure 1, state one storage of matter within the pond ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following:
- Plankton biomass
- Sediment nutrients
- Dissolved oxygen

parts.content: Distinguish between a **transfer** and a **transformation** process, providing one example of each from Figure 1.
parts.markscheme:
- A transfer involves a change in location of matter or energy, whereas a transformation involves a change in the chemical nature or physical state of matter or energy. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples from Figure 1: Transfer = Runoff / Evaporation; Transformation = Respiration (chemical energy to heat) / Photosynthesis (solar radiation to chemical energy stored in plankton). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>2 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain, with reference to Figure 1, why the pond is classified as an **open system**.
parts.markscheme:
- An open system exchanges both energy and matter across its boundary. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 1 shows the exchange of matter via runoff and evaporation, and the exchange of energy via solar radiation and heat loss. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>2 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Suggest **two** advantages of using models, such as the diagram in Figure 1, to study environmental systems.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each valid advantage, up to <marks>2 marks max</marks>:
- They simplify complex systems to make them easier to visualize/understand.
- They allow scientists to make predictions about how the system might respond to changes.
- They help identify the key components (storages) and relationships (flows) within a system.
- They provide a common framework or language for scientists to communicate their findings.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2B Negative feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2B Negative feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) explicitly asks for the role of negative feedback mechanisms in maintaining stable equilibrium in a system.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2C Positive feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2C Positive feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) discusses the role of feedback mechanisms in pushing systems toward tipping points, which is the function of positive feedback loops.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2F Resilience of a system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2F Resilience of a system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) specifically mentions resilience and the transition of systems between states of equilibrium.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) is a detailed evaluation of the use of models, such as systems diagrams and simulations, in understanding ecosystems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) requires knowledge of the soil system, specifically identifying transfers and transformations within it.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) requires understanding of the basic components of a system (storages and matter) applied to an aquatic context.

parts.content: Identify **two** major storages of matter that are present in a named aquatic ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Biomass (e.g., fish, plankton, aquatic plants) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved nutrients (e.g., nitrates, phosphates in the water column) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Benthic sediments (e.g., organic matter or minerals on the lake/sea floor) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved gases (e.g., oxygen, carbon dioxide) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The water body itself (as a storage of $$H_2O$$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid storage identified, up to 2 marks. Do not credit flows (e.g., precipitation or runoff) unless they are clearly defined as a storage within the context of the system.</note>

parts.content: State **one** transfer and **one** transformation of matter that occur within a soil system.
parts.markscheme:
- Transfer: Leaching (minerals moving downwards through soil profiles) / Runoff / Infiltration of water / Deposition of leaf litter <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transformation: Decomposition (organic matter turning into inorganic nutrients) / Weathering of parent rock / Nitrification / Denitrification <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks. Ensure one of each (transfer and transformation) is provided for full marks.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of models (such as systems diagrams or computer simulations) in understanding the complexities of an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Models simplify complex systems, making them easier to visualize and study <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They allow scientists to predict future changes or the impacts of human activities (e.g., climate change models) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They serve as effective communication tools for explaining environmental issues to the public or policy-makers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They can identify gaps in current knowledge or data by highlighting where the model fails to match observations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, models are often over-simplified and may miss emergent properties or small-scale interactions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The accuracy of a model is limited by the quality of the input data ("garbage in, garbage out") <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models often cannot account for unpredictable, stochastic events such as natural disasters <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Predictions are often probabilistic and involve a high degree of uncertainty, which can be misinterpreted <marks>1 mark</marks>



<note>Accept any other reasonable evaluation points. </note>

parts.content:
Environmental systems are often characterized by their resilience and state of equilibrium. 

Discuss the role of feedback mechanisms in determining whether a system maintains a stable equilibrium or crosses a tipping point into a new state.

questions.specification: In environmental science, different modeling approaches can provide insights into various ecological interactions.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to compare quantitative vs qualitative models in environmental science, which directly falls under the ESS foundation concept of models (types, uses, strengths/limitations).

parts.content: Compare and contrast quantitative and qualitative models in environmental science.
parts.markscheme:
Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 4 marks:

Quantitative models use numerical data and mathematical relationships <marks>1 mark</marks>

Qualitative models use descriptive or conceptual relationships <marks>1 mark</marks>

Quantitative models can provide precise predictions or measurements <marks>1 mark</marks>

Qualitative models are useful for understanding complex interactions that are difficult to quantify <marks>1 mark</marks>

Quantitative models may require more data and computational power <marks>1 mark</marks>

Qualitative models may be more suitable for systems with limited data or poorly understood relationships <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The concept of equilibrium (both steady-state and static) is a fundamental characteristic of environmental systems, which is the primary focus of Topic 1.2A.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2B Negative feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2B Negative feedback loops
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Negative feedback loops are the specific mechanisms in environmental systems that allow them to maintain steady-state equilibrium, making it a necessary part of a complete explanation.

parts.content: Explain the concept of equilibrium in environmental systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Equilibrium is a state of balance in a system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In equilibrium, inputs and outputs are balanced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Equilibrium can be static (unchanging) or dynamic (fluctuating around a mean) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Systems tend to move towards equilibrium through feedback mechanisms <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Study the diagram below showing a simple systems model:

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/fafebe9f-b953-4735-b300-f446d7a8820c)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question uses a simple systems model and asks for ecosystem inputs and outputs, which are core systems concepts (inputs/outputs, system boundary).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.1 Individuals, populations, communities and ecosystems > 2.1I Ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.1I Ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It specifically applies the systems model to an ecosystem, so it also draws on the concept of ecosystems as systems with exchanges of energy and matter.

parts.content: Identify two possible inputs for an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Any two from:
  - Solar energy <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Precipitation <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Nutrients <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Carbon dioxide <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Water <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award up to maximum 2 marks total</note>

parts.content: Identify two possible outputs for an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Heat energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Water vapor <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Oxygen <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Organic matter <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Waste products <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award up to maximum 2 marks for any two correct outputs</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2D Models
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The prompt asks generally why models are useful in ESS, which directly targets the foundational systems concept of using models to represent, simplify, and predict environmental systems.

parts.content: Explain why models are useful in environmental systems and societies.
parts.markscheme:
- Models simplify complex systems, making them easier to understand <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models allow for predictions and hypothesis testing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models can represent systems that are too large or complex to study directly <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models can simulate long-term changes or scenarios <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 2 marks.

parts.content: Using the example of global temperature rise and Arctic sea ice, explain how positive feedback mechanisms can lead to the destabilization of a system.
parts.markscheme:
1. **Definition of positive feedback:** A process where an initial change in a system is amplified or increased, leading to further deviation from the original equilibrium. <marks>1 mark</marks>
2. **Initial Trigger:** Rising global temperatures (due to greenhouse gas emissions) cause the melting of Arctic sea ice. <marks>1 mark</marks>
3. **Albedo Effect:** Sea ice has a high albedo (reflectivity), while the dark ocean water has a low albedo. <marks>1 mark</marks>
4. **Mechanism of Change:** As ice melts, more dark ocean surface is exposed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
5. **Absorption of Energy:** The darker ocean absorbs more solar radiation/heat compared to the reflective ice. <marks>1 mark</marks>
6. **Temperature Increase:** This absorption leads to further warming of the local atmosphere and ocean. <marks>1 mark</marks>
7. **Amplification:** The increased warmth leads to even more ice melting, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
8. **Destabilization:** This can push the system toward a tipping point where the Arctic becomes ice-free in summer, representing a new state of equilibrium. <marks>1 mark</marks>
9. **Impact on global systems:** This change can alter global weather patterns/ocean currents, further destabilizing the global climate system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
A scientist creates a sealed, self-sustaining glass biosphere containing producers, consumers, and decomposers, which is then placed in a temperature-controlled room with a constant light source. 

Justify the classification of this biosphere as a **closed system** by describing the behavior of energy and matter within it.
parts.markscheme:
1. **Definition of a closed system:** A system that exchanges energy but not matter across its boundaries. <marks>1 mark</marks>
2. **Energy Flow:** Energy enters the system as light (solar radiation) and leaves the system as heat (long-wave radiation/respiration). <marks>1 mark</marks>
3. **Matter Cycling:** Matter (nutrients, water, carbon, oxygen) remains trapped within the glass boundaries. <marks>1 mark</marks>
4. **Internal Processes:** The producers use the energy to cycle the existing matter (e.g., photosynthesis converting $$CO_2$$ to glucose), which is then used by consumers/decomposers and returned to the system. <marks>1 mark</marks>
5. **Boundary justification:** The glass seal prevents the input of new organic matter or the output of waste products to the external environment. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of quantitative models in predicting tipping points within complex environmental systems.
parts.markscheme:
1. **Definitions and Concepts:** tipping point (the minimum amount of change within a system that will destabilize it, causing it to reach a new equilibrium); quantitative models (mathematical representations of systems using numerical data); resilience; delay in feedback loops; non-linear change. <marks>1 mark</marks>

2. **Strengths of Models:** 
- Allow scientists to simplify complex interactions to identify key drivers of change.
- Can predict future scenarios (e.g., IPCC climate projections) based on current data.
- Enable the testing of "what if" scenarios without harming the actual ecosystem.
- Provide a visual or numerical output that is easier for policymakers to understand. <marks>1 mark</marks>

3. **Limitations of Models:**
- Complex systems often have emergent properties that models cannot account for.
- Quality of the model depends entirely on the accuracy of the input data ("garbage in, garbage out").
- Non-linear changes/tipping points are notoriously difficult to predict because small changes can have disproportionately large effects.
- Models may omit minor variables that become critical as the system nears a threshold. <marks>1 mark</marks>

4. **Examples:** 
- Amazon Rainforest: Models predicting the "dieback" threshold where forest turns to savannah due to reduced transpiration.
- Eutrophication: Models predicting the nutrient loading limit before a lake shifts from clear to turbid state.
- Coral Reefs: Quantitative models of ocean acidification and temperature thresholds for bleaching. <marks>1 mark</marks>

5. **Synthesis/Evaluation:** 
- Models are essential tools for early warning, but they should not be treated as certainties.
- Predicting the exact timing of a tipping point is rarely possible due to the chaotic nature of environmental systems.
- Models are most effective when combined with long-term field observations and the precautionary principle. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Marking should follow the standard IB ESS descriptors for 9-mark questions, focusing on holistic understanding, balanced analysis, and use of relevant examples.</note>

parts.content: Describe two limitations of using models in environmental science.
parts.markscheme:
### Limitation 1
Models may oversimplify complex systems, omitting important factors. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Limitation 2
Models are based on assumptions that may not always be accurate. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**Table 1:** Estimated Annual Global Carbon Budget (approximate annual averages).

| Component | Flow Type | Quantity ($GtC \text{ yr}^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fossil Fuel Combustion | Input to Atmosphere | 9.4 |
| Land-Use Change (e.g., Deforestation) | Input to Atmosphere | 1.5 |
| Terrestrial Vegetation Uptake | Output from Atmosphere | 3.2 |
| Ocean Absorption | Output from Atmosphere | 2.6 |

parts.content: Calculate the net annual accumulation of carbon in the atmosphere based on the data in Table 1.
parts.markscheme:
$$(9.4 + 1.5) - (3.2 + 2.6) = 5.1 \text{ GtC yr}^{-1}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept $5.1$. Units are not required but must be correct if used.

parts.content: Identify two processes from the table that involve a transformation of matter or energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Photosynthesis / Terrestrial Vegetation Uptake (transforms light energy to chemical energy / inorganic $$CO_2$$ to organic biomass)
- Combustion of Fossil Fuels (transforms chemical energy to heat/light / organic carbon to inorganic $$CO_2$$)
- Respiration (if linked to land-use change/decomposition)
- Calcification (if linked to ocean processes)

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following, <marks>2 marks max</marks>.

parts.content: Outline one economic consequence of the continued accumulation of atmospheric carbon.
parts.markscheme:
- Increased costs for disaster relief/infrastructure repair due to extreme weather events
- Financial losses in the agricultural sector due to changing rainfall patterns/droughts
- Rising insurance premiums in high-risk coastal or fire-prone areas
- Costs associated with building coastal defenses/sea walls against rising sea levels
- Loss of revenue from the tourism industry (e.g., bleaching of coral reefs/loss of ski resorts)

<note>Accept any other reasonable economic response. Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any one

parts.content: Outline one environmental implication of the "Ocean Absorption" flow increasing significantly.
parts.markscheme:
- Ocean acidification / decrease in pH of seawater
- Reduced availability of carbonate ions for calcifying organisms (e.g., corals, shellfish)
- Disruption of marine food webs due to loss of sensitive species
- Potential for the ocean to reach a tipping point where it becomes a source rather than a sink

<note>Any one. Do not accept just "global warming" or "sea level rise" as these are atmospheric/thermal effects, not direct consequences of the chemical absorption flow itself.

parts.content: Outline one limitation of using a global carbon model to predict specific local environmental changes.
parts.markscheme:
- Global models lack the resolution/detail to account for local microclimates or topography
- Data for certain regions (e.g., deep oceans or remote rainforests) may be incomplete or estimated
- High level of uncertainty in the magnitude of regional feedback mechanisms
- Human behavior and policy changes are unpredictable and can alter the system's trajectory

<Note> Any one

parts.content: Define the term "carrying capacity" in the context of environmental systems.
parts.markscheme:
<marks>1 mark</marks>

The maximum population size that can be sustainably supported by a given environment

parts.content: Explain how the concept of carrying capacity relates to the logistic growth model.
parts.markscheme:
- The logistic growth model incorporates carrying capacity as an upper limit to population growth <marks>1 mark</marks>

- As population size approaches carrying capacity, growth rate slows <marks>1 mark</marks>

- At carrying capacity, birth and death rates are equal, resulting in a stable population <marks>1 mark</marks>

- The model shows an S-shaped curve, with rapid growth initially slowing as it approaches carrying capacity <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 3 marks</note>

parts.content: Discuss two factors that can cause the carrying capacity of an ecosystem to change over time.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each factor and <marks>1 mark</marks> for its explanation, up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>. Factors could include:

- Climate change: altering habitat conditions and resource availability
- Introduction of new species: competing for resources or altering ecosystem dynamics
- Technological advancements: improving resource use efficiency or exploitation
- Natural disasters: temporarily or permanently altering habitat or resource availability
- Human activities: pollution, habitat destruction, or conservation efforts

parts.content: Evaluate the usefulness and limitations of the carrying capacity concept in environmental management.
parts.markscheme:
### Usefulness

- Provides a framework for understanding population dynamics <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps in setting sustainable harvest or management targets <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aids in assessing the impact of human activities on ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Limitations

- Difficult to determine precisely in complex, dynamic ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May oversimplify the complexities of real-world ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Doesn't account for potential adaptations or evolutionary changes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can be affected by factors external to the immediate ecosystem <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May not apply well to human populations due to technological and cultural influences <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each point, up to a maximum of 4 marks</note>

questions.specification:
**Urban Agriculture in London**
* 9% of London's area is farmland, mostly in the outer green belt
* New focus on vertical farms and rooftop gardens with beekeeping

**Vertical Farming Details:**
* Located underground and in former industrial sites
* Grows salad greens, lettuce, spinach and herbs
* Uses 70% less water and no pesticides versus traditional farms

**London's Bees:**
* 1000+ beekeepers operate in the city
* Higher bee density than rural areas
* City bees are healthier with more diverse food options
* North London features an 11 km wildflower "bee corridor"

**Figure 1: Vertical farm**
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bf35f22a-adce-4787-8797-1aeb0abf4697)


**Figure 2: Rooftop beekeeping**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c59311ba-4f2d-401b-bdc6-3dc15af96f0f)

parts.content: State **one** benefit and **one** drawback of the ecological footprint as a model for measuring sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefit of the ecological footprint model <marks>1 max</marks>

- Good visualizing tool/easily understood by non-specialists
- Environmental indicator/hazard warning indicator
- Widespread use, so easy to compare with other areas
- Highlights problem areas/identifies necessary changes in lifestyle
- Iconic symbol for raising awareness
- Information can be used to inform policy by governments

### Drawback of the ecological footprint model <marks>1 max</marks>

- Calculations are complex/all models are simplifications
- Needs huge amounts of data/uses proxy data/estimates
- Land has more than one function
- Less reliable at local level/not everyone in the region has the same lifestyle
- Can be interpreted differently by cultures
- Does not show types of resources used
- Does not include all factors that contribute to sustainability

<note>Do not accept only 'it is inaccurate/does not use outliers'. Do not accept 'does not account for changes over time'.</note>

parts.content: State **one** factor that would allow a region’s ecological footprint to exceed its biocapacity.
parts.markscheme:
- Importing resources/food
- Exporting waste products

<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Referencing both figures above, suggest how urban agriculture can increase London's carrying capacity
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following; <marks>3 marks max</marks>

- Increases food resources, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- Decreases water use, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- No pesticides, so less water pollution/does not harm/pollute environment / no pesticides used so fewer inputs/resources needed, (which increases carrying capacity) <marks>1</marks>
- Vertical farming uses less land area, (so more land area is available for housing) <marks>1</marks>
- Farming on roofs and in abandoned buildings means more land area is available (for housing/population growth) <marks>1</marks>
- Bees result in more pollination, so more primary productivity, (increasing carbon sink) <marks>1</marks>

<note>Do not accept only 'bees increase resources available' without reference to increasing food.</note>

<note>Do not accept 'bees provide healthier food'.</note>

<note>Do not accept only 'no pesticides used / provides goods / produces agricultural items / pesticides cause eutrophication'.</note>

parts.content: Identify **two** transfers and **two** transformations of energy or matter that occur within a glacier system.
parts.markscheme:
Potential transfers (any two): 
- Movement of ice mass downslope due to gravity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accumulation of snow/precipitation on the glacier surface. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Movement of meltwater through internal channels or out of the snout. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Wind-blown transport of surface debris. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Potential transformations (any two):
- Solid ice melting into liquid water. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Snow compressing and recrystallizing into dense glacial ice (firnification). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sublimation of ice directly into water vapor. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Absorption of solar radiation being converted into heat/internal energy. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each correctly identified process, up to a maximum of 2 for transfers and 2 for transformations.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of a box-and-arrow flow diagram (a model) to represent the storages and flows of a named ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
**Strengths:**
- Simplifies complex biological/geochemical interactions into a format that is easy to visualize. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows for the quantitative comparison of different storages (e.g., biomass vs. litter in a rainforest). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps to identify the most significant pathways of energy or matter loss. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can be used to predict how a change in one component (e.g., removal of a predator) might affect the rest of the system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Limitations:**
- Often oversimplifies the system by excluding minor species or pathways that may still be critical. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Static diagrams do not easily show how flows change over time or with seasons. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The accuracy of the model is entirely dependent on the quality of the initial data collected. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It may ignore the 'human factor' or external unpredictable disturbances (e.g., sudden forest fires). <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Application to named ecosystem:**
- For example, in a Tropical Rainforest: Storages (Biomass, Litter, Soil) and Flows (Leaching, Decomposition, Uptake). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>A maximum of 4 marks may be awarded for strengths and a maximum of 4 marks for limitations. A named ecosystem must be referenced for the highest marks.</note>

parts.content:
"The stability of an environmental system is solely dependent on the presence of negative feedback loops."

Discuss the validity of this statement with reference to named examples of both positive and negative feedback.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:
- Definition of stability/equilibrium (steady-state vs. static).
- Explanation of negative feedback: mechanisms that reverse/dampen a change, bringing the system back to its original state (e.g., predator-prey cycles, temperature regulation in organisms).
- Explanation of positive feedback: mechanisms that amplify change, driving the system toward a tipping point or a new equilibrium (e.g., Albedo effect in the Arctic, methane release from thawing permafrost).
- Discussion on resilience: high biodiversity and complex webs contribute to stability alongside feedback.
- Discussion on how positive feedback can lead to system collapse or a "regime shift" (loss of current stability).

- <marks>2 marks</marks> for a balanced analysis of the extent to which negative feedback is the "sole" factor, acknowledging the role of other factors like ecosystem complexity or the destabilizing influence of positive feedback.

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains: • minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

parts.content: Outline a simple systems diagram for a tropical rainforest ecosystem, including at least three inputs, three outputs, and two storage components.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct overall structure showing inputs $\rightarrow$ storage $\rightarrow$ outputs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At least three correctly **labelled** inputs from: sunlight; precipitation; atmospheric deposition of nutrients; weathering inputs; seed/animal immigration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At least three correctly **labelled** outputs from: evapotranspiration; runoff/streamflow; leaching of nutrients; heat loss; biomass removal (harvesting) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At least two correctly **labelled** storage components from: biomass (vegetation); litter layer; soil organic matter; soil water <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how one feedback mechanism can help to maintain the stability of a tropical rainforest ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a valid feedback mechanism in a tropical rainforest (e.g., increased rainfall $\rightarrow$ increased plant growth $\rightarrow$ increased evapotranspiration $\rightarrow$ increased cloud formation/rainfall; nutrient recycling via litter fall and decomposition supporting primary productivity) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains the sequence of linked processes clearly (cause-and-effect) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links the feedback to maintaining system stability/resilience/homeostasis (e.g., supports productivity, maintains hydrological cycle, maintains nutrient availability) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses appropriate ESS systems terminology (e.g., negative feedback, equilibrium, inputs/outputs/storages/flows) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accepts positive feedback examples if correctly linked to potential instability, provided the explanation addresses system response (OWTTE) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Additional valid detail/accurate example supporting the explanation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion/value judgement on how/why this feedback tends to stabilise the system overall (e.g., “overall this is stabilising because…”) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the claim that tropical rainforest ecosystems are inherently stable and resilient to human disturbance. In your answer, refer to ecological, economic and cultural/political perspectives.
parts.markscheme:
0 marks: The response is not relevant to the question.

1–3 marks: Minimal knowledge of tropical rainforest stability/resilience. Limited or inaccurate use of ESS terminology (e.g., resilience, tipping point). Assertions are largely unlinked and/or descriptive. Little or no evaluation of the claim and/or little reference to perspectives. Examples absent or vague. Conclusion absent or unsupported.

4–6 marks: Some sound knowledge of factors affecting stability/resilience (e.g., biodiversity, nutrient cycling, soil fertility, hydrological cycling) and of human disturbances (e.g., logging, fragmentation, conversion to agriculture, fire). Some evaluation with partially balanced points and some linkage to at least two perspectives (ecological/economic/cultural-political). Uses relevant terminology mostly appropriately. Includes at least one relevant example/case study (can be generic) with limited explanation. Conclusion present but may be limited in support.

7–9 marks: Substantial, well-organised evaluation of the claim with balanced argument (stable in some respects but vulnerable to specific disturbances and thresholds). Integrates ecological, economic, and cultural/political perspectives throughout (e.g., ecosystem services and long-term productivity; short-term economic incentives vs long-term costs; governance/land tenure/indigenous rights and management). Uses precise ESS systems terminology (e.g., resilience, resistance, negative feedback, tipping point, alternative stable states, carrying capacity). Uses well-chosen, well-explained examples (place-based or clearly contextualised). Provides an explicit, well-supported overall judgement about the claim.
